summary: Gruber pushes back against the media narrative that Meta's Threads is failing after losing more than half its initial signups. He argues that a 40-50 percent retention rate for a brand-new MVP app is actually excellent, and that the tech press can only frame stories as binary success or failure. He points to app store rankings showing Threads ahead of X (formerly Twitter) on both the App Store and Play Store as evidence the app is thriving. He notes the app hasn't even launched in the EU yet and lacks basic features like a web interface, making the doom-and-gloom coverage premature.
key_points: A retention drop-off after a buzzy launch is normal and expected — comparing it to a restaurant's grand opening traffic settling down
The tech press defaults to simplistic success/failure narratives, unable to evaluate a new product with nuance
App store rankings on both iOS and Android show Threads ahead of X, contradicting the 'Threads is a bust' framing
Threads launched as an MVP without a web interface, search, or EU availability, making early retention judgments premature
App Store category assignments distort perceived popularity — X sits in 'News' where it looks more popular than it is
money_quotes: A retention rate of 40-50 percent for a new app is excellent.
All you need to do is use Threads to see that it's thriving.

Of course there was going to be a drop-off in usage after the initial influx. When brick-and-mortar stores and restaurants have successful grand openings, no one expects that opening traffic not to drop off.
The idea that an app that is clearly an MVP doesn't have 100 million active users is a failure is the sort of addle brained thinking that the tech media has saddled us with.
The tech press can only write 3 stories: This company is a success. This company is a failure. Look how much funding this company got.
It is a bit depressing that among normal people, web apps are equated with 'desktop'.
Both the App Store and Play Store have Threads ahead of X. You'd never guess that from this weekend's 'Threads is a bust' headlines.
key_insight: The tech press's binary success-or-failure framing led to absurd headlines declaring Threads a bust despite it being a brand-new MVP with tens of millions of daily users and higher app store rankings than X.
